items.title: The transistor inventors - Bell Labs, 1948
items.license: Public Domain (Bell Labs PR photo, PD-US-no-notice)
items.blurb: John Bardeen, William Shockley, Walter Brattain - the three Bell Labs scientists who invented the transistor in December 1947, photographed the next year. Every CPU on Earth descends from this workbench. auge detects all three faces.

items.title: Earthrise - Apollo 8, 24 December 1968
items.blurb: Bill Anders's photograph of Earth rising over the Moon, taken from Apollo 8 on Christmas Eve 1968. The most-reproduced photo of Earth ever taken. Galen Rowell called it 'the most influential environmental photograph ever taken.'

items.title: Banquet of the Officers of the St Hadrian Civic Guard - Frans Hals
items.blurb: Twelve officers at a banquet, Haarlem 1627. Frans Hals founded the Dutch group portrait genre - eyes alive, faces individual. A perfect multi-face benchmark.

items.title: Multilingual sign - Macau
items.blurb: Chinese, Portuguese, and English on a single Macau sign. With --langs, auge handles all three scripts in one OCR pass.

items.title: QR code - what's behind this link?
items.blurb: Most QR codes you scan in the wild, you scan blind. auge decodes the URL on-device first - so you can read the destination before your phone does. Some links are surprises. Some are jokes. This one is a 38-year-old joke that still works.

items.title: The Anatomy Lesson of Dr Nicolaes Tulp - Rembrandt, 1632
items.blurb: Rembrandt at 26, painting the Amsterdam Surgeons' Guild. Eight faces around a dissection - intimate, geometric, masterful. auge detects each one.

items.title: The Blue Marble - Apollo 17, 1972
items.blurb: Apollo 17, 7 December 1972. The crew, the last humans to leave Low Earth Orbit, took the only fully-illuminated photo of Earth made by an astronaut. Vision classifies 'astronomy / planet / globe'.

items.title: Vatican ATM with Latin instructions
items.blurb: The only ATM in the world with a Latin user interface. auge OCRs medieval-screen Latin: 'Inserito scidulam quaeso ut faciundam cognoscas rationem'.

items.title: Abraham Lincoln, 1863
items.blurb: A 19th-century portrait. auge detects the face (with bounding box), classifies the image, and OCRs any visible text.

items.title: Apollo 11 crew portrait, 1969
items.blurb: Armstrong, Collins, Aldrin in pre-flight portrait. NASA published this and every other crew photo into the public domain - try OCR-ing the name tags on the spacesuits. auge does both modes in one pass.

items.title: QR code - Wikipedia URL
items.blurb: A QR code that decodes to Wikipedia's mobile URL. auge --all decodes the barcode and runs every other analysis on the same image, on-device, no camera, no network.

items.title: Pillars of Creation - Hubble, 2014
items.blurb: Three columns of cold gas and dust in the Eagle Nebula, 6,500 light-years from Earth. Hubble first imaged them in 1995; this is the 2014 reshoot at higher resolution. Vision classifies 'astronomy / nebula / sky'.

items.title: Mona Lisa - Leonardo da Vinci, 1503
items.blurb: Probably the most-recognized painting on Earth. auge detects her face and classifies the image - a useful sanity check for what Vision considers a 'painting' vs 'portrait'.

items.title: Domestic cat
items.blurb: Point auge at any image. It returns OCR text (none here), the top classification labels with confidence, faces, and barcodes - all in one pass.

items.title: The Great Wave off Kanagawa - Hokusai, 1831
items.blurb: Hokusai's signature woodblock print from his Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. The most reproduced Japanese artwork ever made. Classify pulls 'art / painting / wave / sea'.

items.title: Girl with a Pearl Earring - Vermeer, 1665
items.blurb: The 'Mona Lisa of the North'. The bounding box should fit her tilted face cleanly - a good test of normalized-coordinate accuracy.

items.title: New-York Daily Tribune, 1865
items.blurb: A real 1865 newspaper PDF from the Internet Archive. auge handles PDF natively via PDFKit and runs every other analysis on each page.

items.title: Welsh-English no-parking sign, Wales
items.blurb: Welsh signage law requires every public sign to be bilingual. auge --langs cy,en-GB OCRs both 'Dim Parcio' and 'No Parking' in one pass.

items.title: Sombrero Galaxy (M104) - Hubble
items.blurb: M104 in Virgo, 28 million light-years away. The dust lane edge-on gives it the namesake hat shape. Vision classifies 'galaxy / astronomy'.

items.title: Marie Curie, c. 1920
items.blurb: The only person ever to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences. auge detects the face and classifies the studio portrait.

items.title: The Kiss - Gustav Klimt, Vienna 1908
items.blurb: Klimt's gold-leaf masterpiece in the Belvedere, Vienna. A genuine challenge for Vision: heavy ornament, abstract gold, two faces partially visible.

items.title: EAN-13 supermarket barcode
items.license: CC0 (locally generated, real ISBN-13 of The Mythical Man-Month)
items.blurb: The barcode you see on every supermarket product. EAN-13 (European Article Number) is the global standard. This one encodes ISBN-13 9780201379624 - The Mythical Man-Month.

items.title: Two women operating ENIAC, 1946
items.blurb: Frances Bilas and Betty Jean Jennings - two of the six women who programmed ENIAC, the first general-purpose digital electronic computer, by physically rewiring it. The original software engineers.

items.title: Hubble Ultra-Deep Field, 2004
items.blurb: Eleven days of exposure in a single patch of sky one-tenth the diameter of a full moon. Roughly 10,000 galaxies, the most distant from when the universe was 800 million years old.

items.title: Goethe in the Roman Campagna - Tischbein, 1787
items.blurb: The icon of Weimar Classicism. Tischbein painted his friend Goethe reclining among Roman ruins - face detection on a 240-year-old oil painting.

items.title: Bird of Washington - John James Audubon, 1838
items.blurb: From Audubon's Birds of America - the most expensive printed book in the world (a complete copy sold for $11.5M in 2010). Hand-coloured engraving of a bird that may not have existed.

items.title: Hunters in the Snow - Pieter Bruegel, 1565
items.blurb: Bruegel's January from his Months series. A complex Flemish winter scene - perfect for stress-testing classification (snow, hunters, dogs, village, ice skating).

items.title: Mozart, by Lange (unfinished, 1782)
items.blurb: Joseph Lange's unfinished oil portrait of his brother-in-law - widely considered the most accurate likeness of Mozart. auge detects the painted face.

items.title: Bilingual French-Dutch street sign, Brussels
items.blurb: Brussels has been officially bilingual since 1932. auge --langs fr-FR,nl-NL handles both scripts on the same sign in one pass.

items.title: QR code - vCard contact
items.blurb: A vCard 3.0 contact card encoded in a QR. auge decodes the multi-line text and you can save it as a contact - all without touching the cloud.

items.title: Eiffel Tower, Paris
items.blurb: 1,083 feet of wrought iron. Classify identifies it - and Vision is good enough to recognise specific landmarks in its training set.

items.title: Beethoven with the Missa solemnis manuscript
items.blurb: Stieler's 1820 portrait. Possible to OCR the manuscript score in his hand? auge runs every analysis to find out.

items.title: Trilingual sign in Jaffa: Arabic, Hebrew, English
items.blurb: Three scripts (Latin, Arabic abjad, Hebrew abjad) on a single street sign in Jaffa. A serious test of right-to-left + multi-script OCR.

items.title: Tournée du Chat Noir - Steinlen, 1896
items.blurb: Steinlen's Belle-Époque poster of a giant black cat over Montmartre. A founding piece of poster art and one of the most-reproduced cat images in history. OCRs the French title.

items.title: The Ambassadors - Hans Holbein, 1533
items.blurb: Two French ambassadors at the court of Henry VIII, surrounded by symbols of learning - and the famous anamorphic skull. Two faces, dense iconography.

items.title: Sterzing / Vipiteno - South Tyrol bilingual sign
items.blurb: South Tyrol road sign: every place name in German and Italian. Sterzing in German, Vipiteno in Italian, same town. auge --langs de-DE,it-IT.

items.title: Anatomy of the Horse - George Stubbs, 1766
items.blurb: George Stubbs spent 18 months dissecting horses to draw them perfectly. The resulting 1766 atlas is half scientific document, half love letter to the animal.

items.title: UPC-A grocery barcode
items.blurb: UPC-A is the 12-digit barcode used on most US groceries. auge --barcode reports it normalised to EAN-13 with a leading zero - same number, same on-device decoder.

items.title: Vienna State Opera House
items.blurb: The Wiener Staatsoper, opened 1869. Neo-Renaissance architecture, classified by auge with crisp confidence.

items.title: QR code on Conwy street info sign (UK, outdoor)
items.blurb: A real-world outdoor QR code on a tourist information sign in Conwy, Wales. auge decodes the URL plus picks up the surrounding sign text.

items.title: Colosseum, Rome
items.blurb: The Flavian Amphitheatre, opened 80 AD. Classify nails 'amphitheatre / ruin / arena' - and auge probably detects faces in the tourist crowd.

items.title: Notre-Dame de Paris, west façade
items.blurb: The Gothic west façade, before the 2019 fire. Sculpture-dense - auge looks for faces in the carved statues alongside cathedral classification.

items.title: The Dodo - George Edwards, 1759
items.blurb: George Edwards's 1759 illustration of a Dodo - an animal already extinct for nearly a century when he drew it, from a Dutch painting. The most charming dead bird in art history.

items.title: Belgrade street sign - Cyrillic and Latin
items.blurb: Serbia uses both Cyrillic and Latin scripts officially. This Belgrade plate names George Washington in both alphabets. auge OCRs sr-Cyrl + sr-Latn at the same time.

items.title: DataMatrix code on a bottle of milk
items.blurb: A real DataMatrix barcode in the wild - on a milk bottle. Vision recognises this format alongside QR, EAN, Code128, PDF417, and Aztec.

items.title: Code 128 industrial barcode
items.blurb: Code 128 encodes ASCII letters and numbers - used on shipping labels, hospital wristbands, and warehouse pallets. Vision decodes all of these one-dimensional symbologies on-device.

items.title: QR code - WiFi network access
items.blurb: Modern QR codes encode WiFi credentials in a single scan. auge --barcode decodes the WIFI: schema URI - on-device, never reaching out to the internet.

items.title: QR code - Bashō haiku
items.blurb: Matsuo Bashō's most famous haiku from 1686, encoded in a QR. auge decodes the multi-line text. The haiku itself is older than the United States.

items.title: Dürer's Rhinoceros (woodcut, 1515)
items.blurb: Dürer's woodcut of an Indian rhinoceros - drawn from a written description, never seen in person. Above it: a Latin inscription. auge OCRs the inscription.

items.title: Gutenberg Bible page (c. 1455)
items.blurb: The first major book printed with movable type in Europe. Latin Vulgate in blackletter, two columns, illuminated. auge OCRs the Latin text with --langs la.

items.title: United States Declaration of Independence, 1776
items.blurb: The 1776 manuscript. Faded ink, dense colonial-era handwriting, irregular line spacing. auge runs every analysis at once.

items.title: James Lyne's New York Survey (Bradford map), c. 1730
items.blurb: An 18th-century map of New York City. Tiny labels, decorative typefaces. Real adversarial input - --enhance upscales tiny lines.

items.title: The Rosetta Stone (196 BC)
items.blurb: The same decree in three scripts - hieroglyphic, demotic Egyptian, ancient Greek. The original multilingual document. auge OCRs the visible Greek section with --langs el-GR.

items.title: Magna Carta - 1215, British Library copy
items.blurb: The 1215 charter that limited the English king's power. Medieval Latin script in dense lines - extreme OCR difficulty. Apple's model attempts it anyway.

items.title: The Last Supper - Leonardo da Vinci, 1498
items.blurb: Leonardo's mural in the refectory of Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan. Thirteen named figures arranged with mathematical precision. auge counts the apostles.

items.title: The School of Athens - Raphael, 1511
items.blurb: Raphael's 1511 fresco in the Vatican: Plato, Aristotle, Socrates, Pythagoras, Euclid, Diogenes - twenty-some philosophers in one room. auge counts the faces and classifies the architecture.

items.title: The Night Watch - Rembrandt, 1642
items.blurb: Rembrandt's monumental civic guard portrait at the Rijksmuseum. Eighteen named figures plus several extras. A genuine stress test for face detection on chiaroscuro paintings.

items.title: Wanderer above the Sea of Fog - Caspar David Friedrich, 1818
items.blurb: The defining image of German Romanticism. A solitary figure above mountain mist - auge tries face detection (back of head, no face visible) and classify on a Romantic landscape.

items.title: The Solvay Conference, Brussels 1927
items.blurb: Twenty-nine of the 20th century's greatest physicists in one frame: Einstein, Curie, Bohr, Heisenberg, Schrödinger, Dirac, Pauli, Lorentz. auge --faces detects and locates them.

items.title: Chinese road sign with QR code (Hainan, outdoor)
items.blurb: A real outdoor QR code in the wild - on a Chinese highway sign. auge decodes the URL plus extracts the Mandarin text alongside it. One image, every analysis.

items.title: Dignity and Impudence - Edwin Landseer, 1839
items.blurb: A sad-eyed bloodhound and a cheeky terrier. Landseer was Queen Victoria's favourite painter. auge classifies the dogs (very accurately) - and may try face-detection on dog faces.

items.title: Hachiko - the loyal Akita, c. 1934
items.blurb: Hachiko, the Akita who waited at Shibuya station every day for nearly ten years after his owner died in 1925. A symbol of loyalty in Japan ever since. auge picks up the dog instantly with --animals.

items.title: Bald Eagle - USFWS
items.blurb: America's national bird, photographed by USFWS. Apple Vision's bird recognition is sharp - and aesthetics scoring rates this kind of wildlife framing highly.

items.title: American Bison - Yellowstone National Park
items.blurb: The largest land animal in North America, in the park that saved it from extinction in the 1890s. Yellowstone's herd is the only continuously wild population in the US.

items.title: Sea Otter Mother with Pup
items.blurb: A southern sea otter mother carrying rare twin pups. Sea otters were hunted to ~50 individuals in California by 1911 - they're back, just barely. Cute index: maximum.

items.title: Laika - first animal in orbit, 1957
items.blurb: Laika, a stray Moscow mongrel, became the first living being to orbit Earth aboard Sputnik 2 on 3 November 1957. She did not survive the flight. The Soviet Union admitted this only in 2002.

items.title: Red Panda
items.blurb: Endangered, native to the eastern Himalayas. Despite the name, not closely related to the giant panda - red pandas are their own family. Mostly bamboo, occasionally insects.

items.title: Cute grey kitten
items.blurb: A baseline test of Apple Vision's animal classifier on what may be the most common photo subject on the open internet.

items.title: Adelie Penguins on iceberg
items.blurb: A line of Adelie penguins on a tabular iceberg in the Ross Sea. They breed only on the Antarctic continent. Climate-change canary species.

items.title: Galapagos giant tortoise
items.blurb: A Galapagos giant tortoise. Darwin spent five weeks on the islands in 1835 - the variation in tortoise shells across islands seeded On the Origin of Species. They live up to 170 years.

items.title: Atlantic Puffin
items.blurb: An Atlantic puffin in breeding plumage - the colourful beak is shed in winter. They mate for life and can hold a dozen fish crosswise in their bill before swallowing.

items.title: Knut - Berlin Zoo polar bear cub
items.blurb: Knut, the polar bear cub born at Berlin Zoo in December 2006 and hand-raised after his mother rejected him. Became a global media phenomenon. Died young, in 2011.
